title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about urban gardens and green spaces. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The City That Grows
content.subtitle: An article about the role of green spaces in urban environments.
content.text:
Sandwiched between a multi-storey car park and a Victorian terrace, the community garden on Allerton Street does not look like much from the outside. A hand-painted sign, a narrow gate, and a tangle of climbing plants signal its existence to those who bother to look. But step through and the noise of the city diminishes, replaced by birdsong, the sound of running water from a small fountain, and the voices of the dozen or so volunteers who tend it every Saturday morning. [31] For those who work here, the garden is not a luxury. It is, as one regular puts it, 'the reason I don't go mad.' [/31]

Urban green spaces — parks, gardens, street trees, green roofs, and planted verges — have become the subject of intense interest among city planners, public health researchers, and environmental scientists. The evidence for their benefits is now substantial. [32] Studies across Europe and North America have consistently found that people living within walking distance of green space report lower levels of stress and anxiety, higher life satisfaction, and improved physical health markers including lower blood pressure and faster recovery from illness. [/32] The mechanisms are debated, but the data is not.

For city planners, however, green space presents a complicated balance sheet. Land in dense urban centres is extraordinarily valuable. Every square metre planted with trees or grass is a square metre not generating tax revenue from development. [33] In cities where housing costs are already prohibitive, the political pressure to build outweighs the health evidence in almost every planning committee that has ever sat. [/33] Green space, unless it has powerful advocates, tends to lose.

The argument is shifting, however, as economists begin to quantify what had previously been considered unquantifiable. [34] When researchers in Manchester calculated the healthcare savings attributable to reduced stress and anxiety among residents living near parks, the figure came to hundreds of millions of pounds over a decade — far exceeding the development value of the land. [/34] Green space, properly accounted for, begins to look like an investment rather than an indulgence.

There is also the question of climate. Urban trees reduce local temperatures by several degrees through shade and evapotranspiration, lowering the heat island effect that makes cities dangerous during heatwaves. They absorb rainfall, reducing flood risk. They filter particulates from the air. [35] Individually, each tree contributes modestly; collectively, a well-planted city can reduce its peak summer temperature by enough to prevent hundreds of heat-related deaths annually. [/35]

Back on Allerton Street, the volunteers are not thinking about heat island effects or healthcare economics. They are planting broad beans, arguing gently about whether the apple tree needs pruning, and sharing a thermos of tea. [36] What they have created is a place of radical ordinariness — an unremarkable patch of earth that turns out, on reflection, to be doing several extraordinary things at once. [/36]

questions.question: The volunteer who says the garden is 'the reason I don't go mad' is suggesting that
options: A) city life is overwhelming and the garden provides essential psychological relief.
B) the work of gardening is so demanding it prevents boredom.
C) the garden provides a source of income that reduces financial stress.
D) urban gardens should be made available to everyone, not just volunteers.
questions.explanation: The quote, combined with the contrast between the garden's calm and the city's noise, shows the garden provides essential psychological relief from stressful city life.

questions.question: What does the research mentioned in the second paragraph show?
options: A) Green spaces are most beneficial when they include running water.
B) People who live near green spaces have measurably better health outcomes.
C) Green spaces only benefit people who actively participate in gardening.
D) The mental health benefits of green spaces are greater than the physical ones.
questions.explanation: The studies found 'lower levels of stress and anxiety, higher life satisfaction, and improved physical health markers' for people living near green spaces — measurably better outcomes.

questions.question: According to the writer, the main obstacle to protecting urban green spaces is
options: A) a lack of scientific evidence for their benefits.
B) the high cost of maintaining parks and gardens.
C) the economic pressure to use valuable land for development.
D) public indifference to the importance of nature in cities.
questions.explanation: The writer explains that 'the political pressure to build outweighs the health evidence' because urban land is 'extraordinarily valuable' for development.

questions.question: The Manchester research is cited to demonstrate that
options: A) parks in Manchester are particularly well designed.
B) the financial value of green spaces can exceed the value of developing the land.
C) healthcare costs in cities without green spaces are rising rapidly.
D) economists are more reliable than planners when assessing green space.
questions.explanation: The research showed healthcare savings that 'far exceeded the development value of the land', proving green space can be worth more financially than development.

questions.question: The writer's point about urban trees in the fifth paragraph is that
options: A) a single tree has a negligible effect on the urban environment.
B) tree planting is only effective in cities with existing parks.
C) the combined effect of many trees can significantly reduce climate-related deaths.
D) trees are more effective than other solutions to the urban heat island problem.
questions.explanation: The text says 'Individually, each tree contributes modestly; collectively, a well-planted city can...prevent hundreds of heat-related deaths annually', emphasising the combined effect.

questions.question: The phrase 'radical ordinariness' in the final paragraph suggests that the garden is
options: A) remarkable precisely because of how simple and everyday it appears.
B) politically significant as a protest against urban development.
C) an unusual type of garden that differs from conventional designs.
D) misunderstood by the volunteers who work there every week.
questions.explanation: 'Radical ordinariness' — seeming unremarkable but 'doing several extraordinary things at once' — means the garden is remarkable because of its apparent simplicity.
